reflection.theme: The Saturday You Couldn't Have Before
reflection.quote: The afternoon knows what the morning never suspected.
reflection.reading:
Late May Saturdays used to terrify me. Not in any dramatic way — just the slow dread of an open day with nothing to numb. I'd wake up already negotiating with the afternoon, trying to figure out where the drinking would start and who would be there to make it look normal. The day wasn't a day. It was a container for the next blackout.

What nobody told me at 22 was that getting sober doesn't shrink the Saturday. It hands it back to you. A whole unscheduled day in late spring — the kind of light that makes you want to be outside, a coffee that actually tastes like something, a friend who texts to see if you want to go for a walk and means it. That used to sound like a punishment. Now it's the entire point. The life I was scared of losing was the one I didn't have yet.

Frost was right: the afternoon knows things the morning can't. The version of me drinking on a Saturday morning couldn't have imagined this one — eleven years in, no hangover, the day my own. Presence isn't a discipline you white-knuckle. It's what's left when you stop running. Today, whatever it holds, is the day. Show up for it. That's the whole assignment.
reflection.meditation: Where in today is there an opening for me to simply be present, instead of escaping into the next thing?

affirmations: I am capable of meeting an unstructured day without needing to numb it.
Today I get to build a life I don't have to recover from on Monday.
I am present for the ordinary hours, and that is where my real life happens.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for a Saturday I can remember. Grateful for the coffee, the light, the quiet, and the people who text me back. Grateful that the version of me who couldn't imagine this day isn't running things anymore — but he got me here, and I owe him this.
gratitude.closing: One day. This one. That's enough.
